turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Jackson has the opinion of the Court this morning in case 21-1436, Santos-Zacaria v. Garland.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: In the immigration context, a non-citizen in removal proceedings can argue her case before several levels of agency decision makers before proceeding to Court. In fact going through the agency review process is required at least to some extent. This is known as an Exhaustion Requirement. This case requires us to interpret 8 U.S.C. Section 1252(d)(a) the statutory exhaustion requirement that governs removal proceedings. Petitioner Leon Santos-Zacaria who goes by the name Estrella is a non-citizen facing removal from this country. She sought protection from removal first before an immigration judge and then in an agency appeal to the Board of Immigration Appeals. When the board ruled against her, she did not ask the board to reconsider its decision. Instead, she sought judicial review in the US Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit. That Court held that Santos-Zacaria could not proceed in court because she had not satisfied the statutory exhaustion requirement. Two aspects of the Fifth Circuit's decision are currently at issue. First, the Fifth Circuit raised the exhaustion rule on its own even though no party had invoked the matter. The Court believed that exhaustion is a jurisdictional requirement, which a party cannot waive or forfeit. Second, the Fifth Circuit held that Santos-Zacaria failed to exhaust because she had not sought reconsideration from the Board of Immigration Appeals. Today, we hold that the Court of Appeals was mistaken on both counts. First, we hold that 1252(d)(1)'s exhaustion requirement is not jurisdictional. A jurisdictional rule sets the bounds of the courts adjudicatory authority that label carries harsh consequences, and thus this Court requires a clear statement in the statute about its jurisdictional nature before we treat a rule as jurisdictional. The provision before us 1252(d)(1) lacks the necessary clear statement. For one thing, exhaustion requirements are quintessential non-jurisdictional claim processing rules. In addition, this provisions language differs substantially from more clearly jurisdictional language nearby in the statute. In several related provisions, Congress specified that ?no Court shall have jurisdiction? to review certain matters. Yet the language of 1252(d)(1) breaks from that mold. Taken together, these two features the fact that this is an exhaustion provision and that Congress used plainly jurisdictional language in related provisions establish that this exhaustion requirement is an ordinary claim processing rule not a limit on the Court's Jurisdiction. Second, we hold that a non-citizen need not seek reconsideration from the Board of Immigration Appeals in order to exhaust under 1252(d)(1). Per that statute, a non-citizen must exhaust only those administrative remedies that are available to the non-citizen ?as of right?. In the context relevant here, review of a legal claim the phrase as of right means review that is guaranteed, not discretionary. But board reconsideration is discretionary. Therefore, it is not available as of right and Section 1252(d)(1) does not require a non-citizen to pursue it. Santos-Zacaria did not ask the board to reconsider its decision but that does not bar a Court from reviewing her case. For these reasons and others explained in the Court's opinion we vacate the relevant portion of the judgment below. Justice Alito has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment in which Justice Thomas joins.
